# Score: -274.875
# True score -274.875
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Get the number of nodes
    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    
    # Calculate the row-wise sum of distances (simplified version of inverse-distance weight)
    row_sums = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1)
    
    # Normalize row sums to sum up to 1
    row_sums_norm = row_sums / row_sums.sum()
    
    # Calculate the effective distance for each edge
    heuristic_matrix = distance_matrix + row_sums_norm[:, np.newaxis] - np.eye(num_nodes)
    
    return heuristic_matrix

from scipy.optimize import linear_sum_assignment
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the inverse of distances
    inverse_distances = 1 / distance_matrix

    # Avoid negative or zero values (since we're dealing with distances)
    inverse_distances[inverse_distances < 0] = 0

    # Scale the inverse distances to sum up to 1
    total = inverse_distances.sum()
    if total!= 0:
        inverse_distances *= 1 / total

    return inverse_distances

# Score: -312.687
# True score -312.687
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)

    # For each node pair (i, j)
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i + 1, n):  # Avoid self-loops
            # Compute the cost of adding the edge (i, j)
            cost_ij = distance_matrix[i, j]

            # Compute the cost of creating a cycle (i, j)
            cycle_cost = max(distance_matrix[i, k] for k in range(n) if k!= j) + \
                         max(distance_matrix[j, l] for l in range(n) if l!= i)

            # Set the heuristic value for (i, j)
            heuristic_matrix[i, j] = cost_ij + cycle_cost

    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -294.368
# True score -294.368
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Compute the cost-to-traverse score for each edge
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    cost_to_traverse = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)
    
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            # Consider direction and number of hops to get from i to j
            cost_to_traverse[i, j] = distance_matrix[i, j] + 1
            if i < j:  # Traversing from i to j is more costly
                cost_to_traverse[i, j] += 1  # Add 1 for every hop in the reverse direction

    # Combine distance with cost-to-traverse
    heuristic_matrix = distance_matrix + cost_to_traverse
    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -342.402
# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Adding an indirect influence factor using the average distance of adjacent edges
    indirect_factor = np.mean(distance_matrix, axis=0)  # For each vertex

    # Normalize the direct distance for better comparability with indirect factor
    direct_distances = np.divide(distance_matrix, np.linalg.norm(distance_matrix, ord=1, axis=0), out=np.zeros_like(distance_matrix), where=distance_matrix!=0)

    # Create the new heuristics matrix
    heuristics_matrix = direct_distances - indirect_factor  # Penalize edges with large direct distances and average adjacent distances

    return heuristics_matrix

# True score -319.02
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the degree of each node (number of neighbors)
    degrees = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=0)
    
    # Create a weight matrix where each entry represents the weight of the corresponding edge
    weight_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)
    
    for i in range(distance_matrix.shape[0]):
        for j in range(distance_matrix.shape[1]):
            weight_matrix[i, j] = 1 / degrees[j]  # More connected nodes should have lower weights
    
    # Normalize weights to get a probability distribution
    weight_matrix = weight_matrix / np.sum(weight_matrix, axis=0)
    
    return weight_matrix

# Score: -316.441
# True score -316.441
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Initialize the heuristic matrix with the same shape as the distance matrix
    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros((num_nodes, num_nodes))

    # Edge density calculation
    edge_density = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1) / (num_nodes - 1)
    heuristic_matrix += edge_density[:, np.newaxis] + edge_density[np.newaxis, :] - 2 * distance_matrix

    # Add edge weight normalization
    min_distance = np.min(distance_matrix)
    heuristic_matrix += (min_distance - distance_matrix) / min_distance

    # Normalization
    max_heuristic = np.max(heuristic_matrix)
    heuristic_matrix = (heuristic_matrix - np.min(heuristic_matrix)) / (max_heuristic - np.min(heuristic_matrix))

    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -309.366
# True score -309.366
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Get the shape of the distance matrix
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]

    # Initialize an array of zeros with the same shape as the distance matrix
    heuristics_matrix = np.zeros(distance_matrix.shape)

    # Iterate through each vertex
    for i in range(n):
        # Calculate the sum of the unvisited distances (current and its closest unvisited vertex)
        min_distance = float('inf')
        min_index = -1
        for j in range(n):
            if distance_matrix[i, j]!= 0:  # Avoid dividing by zero
                if j!= i:  # Ignore the edge between i and itself
                    if distance_matrix[i, j] < min_distance:
                        min_distance = distance_matrix[i, j]
                        min_index = j

        # If the vertex has unvisited neighbors, assign the minimum distance
        if min_index!= -1:
            heuristics_matrix[i, min_index] = min_distance

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -322.789
# True score -322.789
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the square of the distance for each element
    squared_distances = np.square(distance_matrix)
    
    # Calculate the row sums to get the total distance for each node
    row_sums = np.sum(squared_distances, axis=1)
    
    # Calculate the distance density (sum of squared distances from each node)
    distance_density = row_sums - squared_distances
    
    return distance_matrix + distance_density

# Score: -334.951
# True score -334.951
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the inverse of distance matrix
    inv_dist_matrix = 1 / distance_matrix
    
    # Normalize the inverse matrix to sum up to 1 along each row
    row_sums = np.sum(inv_dist_matrix, axis=1)
    normalized_inv_dist_matrix = inv_dist_matrix / row_sums[:, np.newaxis]
    
    # Return the normalized inverse distance matrix as heuristic scores
    return normalized_inv_dist_matrix

# Score: -304.488
# True score -304.488
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Ensure the input is a valid distance matrix
    if not np.allclose(distance_matrix, distance_matrix.T) or np.any(distance_matrix < 0):
        raise ValueError("Input must be a symmetric, non-negative distance matrix.")
    
    # Calculate normalized distances
    normalized_distances = distance_matrix / np.max(distance_matrix)
    
    # Add a simple path length penalty
    path_length = np.sum(normalized_distances, axis=1)
    path_length_penalty = 0.1 * path_length
    
    # Add a priority score based on the immediate neighborhood
    n, m = distance_matrix.shape
    priority_scores = np.zeros((n, m))
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(m):
            # Consider the 8 immediate neighbors (up, down, left, right, and four diagonals)
            neighbors_dist = np.minimum(distance_matrix[i], distance_matrix[j])
            neighbors_penalty = 0.5 * (1 - neighbors_dist)
            priority_scores[i, j] = np.sum(neighbors_penalty)
    
    # Combine all components
    heuristic_values = (1 - 0.1) * normalized_distances + path_length_penalty + priority_scores
    
    # Ensure the heuristic values are non-negative
    heuristic_values[heuristic_values < 0] = 0
    
    return heuristic_values

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Threshold for undesirable edges
    UNDESIRABLE_THRESHOLD = 100

    # Convert negative values (costly edges) to their absolute values
    distance_matrix = np.abs(distance_matrix)

    # Create an undesirable edge penalty matrix
    undesirable_penalty = UNDESIRABLE_THRESHOLD * np.ones(distance_matrix.shape)

    # Distribute penalty to costly edges
    for i in range(distance_matrix.shape[0]):
        for j in range(distance_matrix.shape[1]):
            if distance_matrix[i, j] < UNDESIRABLE_THRESHOLD:
                undesirable_penalty[i, j] = 0  # No penalty for low-cost edges

    return undesirable_penalty

# True score -343.136
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Convert distance matrix to connectivity matrix (adjacency matrix)
    connectivity_matrix = np.ones((len(distance_matrix), len(distance_matrix))) - distance_matrix
    # Compute the inverse of distances
    inverse_distances = 1 / distance_matrix
    # Adjust for edge density and length
    edge_density = np.mean(distance_matrix)  # Use average distance as a simple measure of edge density
    connectivity_weight = inverse_distances * edge_density
    heuristics_matrix = connectivity_matrix * connectivity_weight
    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -336.406
# True score -336.406
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Get the shape of the input matrix
    n, _ = distance_matrix.shape
    
    # Initialize a similar shaped matrix to hold the heuristic values
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)
    
    # Apply the Inverse Distance Heuristic
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            if i!= j:
                # Calculate the inverse of the distance
                inv_distance = 1 / distance_matrix[i, j]
                # Assign the inverse distance as the heuristic value
                heuristic_matrix[i, j] = inv_distance
    
    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -342.402
# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Negate distances to turn higher values into lower ones
    neg_distance_matrix = -distance_matrix
    # Get the unique vertices
    n = neg_distance_matrix.shape[0]
    # Initialize the result matrix with zeros
    result = np.zeros_like(neg_distance_matrix)
    
    # Kruskal's Algorithm
    tree = []
    visited = set()
    edge_weights = list(zip(np.arange(n), np.arange(n)))
    
    while len(tree) < n - 1:
        # Find the smallest edge
        min_weight = float('inf')
        min_edge = None
        for i, j in edge_weights:
            if i not in visited and j not in visited:
                if neg_distance_matrix[i, j] < min_weight:
                    min_weight = neg_distance_matrix[i, j]
                    min_edge = (i, j)
        # Include the smallest edge in the tree
        tree.append(min_edge)
        visited.add(min_edge[0])
        visited.add(min_edge[1])
        
        # Check if adding this edge forms a cycle
        for k, l in edge_weights:
            if k in visited and l in visited and min_edge[0]!= k and min_edge[1]!= l:
                continue
            if neg_distance_matrix[k, l] < min_weight:
                edge_weights.remove((k, l))

    # Fill the result matrix with the weights of the edges in the tree
    for i, j in tree:
        result[i, j] = neg_distance_matrix[i, j]
    
    return result

The most popular heuristic for MST is Kruskal's Algorithm or Prim's Algorithm. Here, I'll use Kruskal's Algorithm, which sorts all edges in increasing order of weight and then includes them into a tree until it has `n-1` edges, where `n` is the number of vertices.\n\nIn this implementation, a lower score means the edge is less desirable to include in the solution. # True score -277.537
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Compute the sum of the row elements for each row as a weight
    row_sums = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1)
    
    # Compute the inverse of these row sums (to 'normalize' them)
    weights = 1 / row_sums

    # Create an importance matrix by multiplying the inverse row sums with the original distance matrix
    heuristics_matrix = distance_matrix * weights[:, np.newaxis]

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -329.12
# True score -329.12
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Compute degree of each node (number of edges connected to it)
    degrees = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=0)
    
    # Adjust degrees to reflect the impact on flow conservation
    # We'll adjust upwards by a certain multiplier, typically between 1 and 2
    adjusted_degrees = np.maximum(1, degrees * 1.2)
    
    # Normalize the adjusted degrees to be between 0 and 1
    normalized_degrees = adjusted_degrees / np.max(adjusted_degrees)
    
    # Compute the heuristic score matrix
    heuristic_score = np.outer(normalized_degrees, normalized_degrees) - distance_matrix
    
    return heuristic_score

# Score: -342.402
# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Normalize the distance matrix
    max_distance = np.max(distance_matrix)
    distance_matrix = distance_matrix / max_distance

    # Calculate pairwise product
    product_matrix = np.multiply(distance_matrix, distance_matrix)

    # Incorporate a penalty for large distances
    large_distances_penalty = 1 - np.exp(-product_matrix / 10)
    large_distances_penalty[large_distances_penalty < 0.5] = 0

    # Asymmetry penalty for distances that are not the same
    asymmetry_penalty = np.abs(distance_matrix - np.transpose(distance_matrix))

    # Combine the penalties
    heuristics_matrix = large_distances_penalty + asymmetry_penalty

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -315.143
# True score -315.143
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Compute the total weight of connections
    total_weight = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=0)

    # Compute local density for each node
    N = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    densities = np.zeros(N)
    for i in range(N):
        neighbors = np.argwhere(distance_matrix[i]!= 0).flatten()
        if len(neighbors) > 0:  # Avoid division by zero
            densities[i] = 2 * np.sum(distance_matrix[i, neighbors]) / np.sum(distance_matrix[i, neighbors]**2)
        else:
            densities[i] = 0

    # Combine these into a heuristic matrix
    heuristic_matrix = -distance_matrix / total_weight
    heuristic_matrix += 1 / densities  # Increase weights for denser nodes

    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -336.406
# True score -336.406

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros(distance_matrix.shape)
    
    for i in range(num_nodes):
        for j in range(i+1, num_nodes):
            distance = distance_matrix[i, j]
            heuristic_matrix[i, j] = heuristic_matrix[j, i] = 1 / distance  # Assuming the same distance in both directions
    
    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -275.106
# True score -275.106
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Normalize the distance matrix to [0, 1] range
    min_distance = np.min(distance_matrix)
    max_distance = np.max(distance_matrix)
    normalized_matrix = (distance_matrix - min_distance) / (max_distance - min_distance)
    
    # Create an initial heuristics matrix
    heuristics_matrix = normalized_matrix.copy()
    
    # Assign higher values to shorter distances
    for i in range(distance_matrix.shape[0]):
        for j in range(distance_matrix.shape[1]):
            if i < j:  # Only consider direct edges
                heuristics_matrix[i][j] = normalized_matrix[i][j] * (1 - distance_matrix[i][j] / max_distance)  # More weight for shorter distances
    
    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -316.683
# True score -316.683
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros((n, n))
    
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            if i == j:  # Avoid self-loops
                continue
            avg_distance = np.mean(distance_matrix[i, :]) + np.mean(distance_matrix[:, j])
            heuristic_matrix[i, j] = avg_distance  # Higher risk indicates less desirable edge
    
    return heuristic_matrix

import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Initialize the heuristic matrix with the same shape as the distance matrix
    heuristics_matrix = np.copy(distance_matrix)
    
    # Number of nodes
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    
    # Compute the negative of the distance matrix to represent potential cost
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            if i!= j:
                heuristics_matrix[i, j] = -distance_matrix[i, j]  # Negate distance to represent potential cost

    # Compute a density factor to account for clustering
    # In this example, a simple approximation is used where the density is 1/n
    # Adjust this factor according to specific requirements of your problem
    density = 1 / n
    
    # Apply density factor to the heuristic matrix
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            if i!= j:
                heuristics_matrix[i, j] *= density
    
    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -304.745
# True score -304.745
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the average distance for each node
    avg_distances = np.mean(distance_matrix, axis=1)
    
    # Calculate the divergence of each node from its average distance
    # This divergence is the absolute difference from the mean
    divergences = np.abs(avg_distances - np.mean(avg_distances))
    
    # The lower the divergence, the closer the node is to its average,
    # implying that removing the node's edges would have less impact
    # on the overall connectivity.
    # Divergences act as a measure of node importance and potential impact.
    
    # Apply this to each edge by multiplying divergences with distances
    heuristics_matrix = divergences[:, None] * distance_matrix
    
    return heuristics_matrix

import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Initialize the heuristic matrix with the same shape as the distance matrix
    heuristic_matrix = np.copy(distance_matrix)
    
    # Calculate the negative sum of weights along the diagonal for each node
    for i in range(heuristic_matrix.shape[0]):
        heuristic_matrix[i][i] = -np.sum(distance_matrix[i])
    
    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -289.104
# True score -289.104

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros((num_nodes, num_nodes))

    # Calculate an edge's importance based on its position
    for i in range(num_nodes):
        for j in range(i, num_nodes):
            dist = distance_matrix[i, j]  # This is already provided in the input
            heuristic_matrix[i, j] = dist
            heuristic_matrix[j, i] = dist  # To avoid over-counting, also assign the same importance to reverse edges

    # Weights can be adjusted based on the specific characteristics of the problem
    # Here, we assign higher weights to edges connecting nodes with more neighbors
    weights = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1) / num_nodes  # Node i's total weighted distance to all other nodes
    heuristic_matrix *= weights

    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -293.088
# True score -293.088
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    """
    This function assigns weights to each edge in the distance_matrix,
    using a weighted shortest path approach. Edges with larger distances
    are assigned smaller weights, making them less desirable.

    Args:
    distance_matrix: A 2D numpy array where distance_matrix[i][j] represents
                    the distance between nodes i and j.

    Returns:
    A 2D numpy array of the same shape as the input, where each entry
    represents the heuristic weight for the corresponding edge in the
    distance_matrix.
    """
    # Check if the input is a 2D numpy array
    if not isinstance(distance_matrix, np.ndarray) or distance_matrix.ndim!= 2:
        raise ValueError("Input must be a 2D numpy array.")
    
    # Calculate the number of nodes
    n_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]

    # Calculate the weight for each edge
    weights = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)
    for i in range(n_nodes):
        for j in range(n_nodes):
            if i!= j:  # Avoid self-loops
                # Use the formula for the WSP heuristic: sqrt(distance)
                weights[i][j] = np.sqrt(distance_matrix[i][j])

    return weights

import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = len(distance_matrix)
    heuristics_matrix = np.copy(distance_matrix)  # Initialize the heuristics matrix

    # Assuming the current shortest path is already provided as'shortest_path'
    #'shortest_path' should be a list of indices representing the shortest path
    # in the original matrix
    shortest_path = [i for i in range(n)]  # Example: Assume the shortest path is all nodes

    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            if i!= j and (i, j) in shortest_path:  # For edges in the shortest path
                heuristics_matrix[i][j] = np.inf  # Set these edges to infinity
                heuristics_matrix[j][i] = np.inf  # Symmetric, so also set the reverse edge
            elif (i, j) not in shortest_path:  # For edges not in the shortest path
                heuristics_matrix[i][j] = distance_matrix[i][j]  # Set these edges to their original distances

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -336.854

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the distance between each pair of nodes
    num_nodes = len(distance_matrix)
    distances = numpy.triu(distance_matrix) # Consider only above diagonal elements for lower-triangular matrix
    row_ind, col_ind = numpy.where(distances > 0)

    # Define a risk factor as the absolute difference between distances divided by the maximum distance
    risk_factor = abs(distances[row_ind, col_ind]) / distance_matrix.max()

    # Create a heuristic matrix, where the diagonal elements are 0 (no cost) and higher off-diagonal elements represent riskier choices
    heuristic_matrix = numpy.zeros((num_nodes, num_nodes))
    heuristic_matrix[row_ind, col_ind] = risk_factor[row_ind] + risk_factor[col_ind]

    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -277.537
# True score -277.537
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]  # Assuming square distance matrix
    avg_weights = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1)  # Average weight of edges connected to each node
    wndh_scores = distance_matrix / avg_weights[:, np.newaxis]  # Normalize by average edge weights

    # Ensure scores are non-negative (this is actually a workaround for a possible division by zero,
    # as theoretically, each node should be connected to at least one other node)
    wndh_scores[np.isinf(wndh_scores)] = 0

    return wndh_scores

# Score: -314.934
# True score -314.934
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    manhattan = np.abs(distance_matrix - np.triu(distance_matrix, k=1))
    euclidean = np.sqrt(np.sum((distance_matrix - np.diag(np.ones(n)))**2, axis=1))
    max_distance = np.max(euclidean)
    
    # Reciprocal of the maximum distance
    max_distance_reciprocal = 1 / max_distance
    
    # Create a new matrix to store our new heuristic indicators
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros(distance_matrix.shape)
    
    # Apply the Manhattan Distance
    heuristic_matrix += manhattan
    
    # Apply the Euclidean Distance
    heuristic_matrix += euclidean
    
    # Apply the Reciprocal of the Maximum Distance
    heuristic_matrix += max_distance_reciprocal
    
    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -298.795
# True score -298.795
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Determine the size of the matrix (number of nodes)
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]

    # Initialize the heuristic matrix with zeros
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)

    # Compute the heuristic value for each edge (i, j)
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            # Exclude diagonal elements (loop back to the same node)
            if i!= j:
                # The heuristic for edge (i, j) is the minimum possible cost for connecting i and j
                heuristic_matrix[i, j] = distance_matrix[i, j]  # This is the direct distance if edges were straight lines
                # However, we should also consider a potential'shortcut' cost, so we add a value slightly greater than the max possible cost
                heuristic_matrix[i, j] += 1000  # You can adjust this number based on your specific use case

    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -294.903
# True score -294.903
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = len(distance_matrix)
    # Step 1: Nearest Neighbor heuristic
    nn_heuristics = np.minimum.accumulate(distance_matrix, axis=1)
    
    # Step 2: 2-Opt heuristic (Finding the shortest cycle in the current partial tour)
    partial_tour = np.argsort(np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1), axis=0)
    current_tour_distances = np.sum(distance_matrix[partial_tour])
    best_cycle_distance = current_tour_distances
    
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i + 1, n):
            if np.all(np.array([i, j, partial_tour[i], partial_tour[j]])!= np.array([i, j, partial_tour[j], partial_tour[i]])):  # Avoid repeated edges
                cycle_distances = np.sum(distance_matrix[partial_tour[i:j+1]])
                if cycle_distances < best_cycle_distance:
                    best_cycle_distance = cycle_distances
    
    # Step 3: Combine NN and 2-Opt heuristics
    heuristics_matrix = np.minimum(nn_heuristics, best_cycle_distance)
    return heuristics_matrix

# True score -275.475
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Get the shape of the input matrix
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]

    # Compute the distance to the origin
    origin_distance = np.min(distance_matrix, axis=0)

    # Compute the cost-to-travel heuristic for each edge
    heuristics_matrix = distance_matrix - origin_distance[:, np.newaxis]

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -342.402
# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    # Create adjacency matrix with edge weights
    adj_matrix = np.abs(distance_matrix - np.triu(distance_matrix, k=1))

    # Initialize the heuristics matrix with the same shape as the distance matrix
    heuristics_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)

    # Apply the heuristic: edge weight >= 0, if edge is not part of a minimum spanning tree
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i + 1, n):
            # Exclude the diagonal for non-self-loops
            if adj_matrix[i, j] > 0:
                heuristics_matrix[i, j] = adj_matrix[i, j]
                heuristics_matrix[j, i] = adj_matrix[i, j]

    return heuristics_matrix

def heuristics(distance_matrix):

    heuristic_matrix = avg_distance * np.ones(distance_matrix.shape)

# Score: -340.322
# True score -340.322

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    num_vertices = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    # Calculate edge importance by taking the inverse of squared distances
    edge_importance = 1 / (distance_matrix ** 2).sum(axis=1)
    # Normalize importance values so they sum to 1 for each vertex
    normalized_importance = edge_importance / edge_importance.sum()

    # Create and return the heuristics matrix
    heuristics_matrix = -normalized_importance.reshape(-1, 1)
    return heuristics_matrix

import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the decay based on distance
    max_distance = np.max(distance_matrix)
    decay_factor = np.exp(-(distance_matrix / max_distance))

    # Ensure no weight goes below 1
    decay_factor = np.minimum(decay_factor, 1)

    # The new heuristics matrix
    heuristics_matrix = decay_factor * distance_matrix

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -309.197
# True score -309.197
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = len(distance_matrix)
    # Calculate the weighted average distance
    weighted_avg = np.average(distance_matrix, axis=0)
    # Calculate the second-order deviation
    second_order_deviation = np.square(distance_matrix - weighted_avg)
    # Calculate the total weighted deviation
    total_deviation = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=0) + np.sum(second_order_deviation)
    # Return the total weighted deviation as the heuristic values
    return total_deviation.reshape(-1, 1)

import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Subtract the diagonal from each row for edge bias
    distance_matrix -= np.diag(np.diag(distance_matrix))

    # Multiply by a weight for edge importance
    weight_matrix = np.exp(-distance_matrix / np.max(np.abs(distance_matrix)))

    # Adjust the values for edge bias
    adjusted_matrix = distance_matrix + weight_matrix * 2  # The '2' is an arbitrary scaling factor, can be tuned

    return adjusted_matrix

# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate average degree of nodes
    avg_degree = np.mean(np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1))

    # Create a new distance matrix where each edge's score is its weight minus the average degree
    undesirable_edges = distance_matrix - avg_degree

    # Ensure the resulting matrix is non-negative, as edge scores should not be negative
    undesirable_edges = np.maximum(undesirable_edges, 0)

    return undesirable_edges

# Score: -338.673
# True score -338.673
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the immediate cost
    immediate_cost = distance_matrix
    
    # Compute potential future savings
    # We'll use a simple 'distance to centroid' metric for this example
    centroid_distances = np.mean(distance_matrix, axis=0)
    potential_savings = distance_matrix - centroid_distances[:, np.newaxis]
    
    # Combine immediate cost and potential savings to create a new heuristic matrix
    heuristic_matrix = immediate_cost + potential_savings
    
    # Normalize the heuristic matrix so all values are between 0 and 1
    max_heuristic = np.max(heuristic_matrix)
    normalized_heuristic_matrix = heuristic_matrix / max_heuristic
    
    return normalized_heuristic_matrix

# Score: -309.993
# True score -309.993
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    num_nodes, _ = distance_matrix.shape
    
    # Calculate the average distance
    avg_distance = np.mean(distance_matrix)
    
    # Density factor (1 for sparse, >1 for dense regions)
    density = 1 / np.count_nonzero(distance_matrix > 0)
    
    # Edge weight factor
    edge_weights_proportion = distance_matrix / np.linalg.norm(distance_matrix, axis=1)[:, np.newaxis]
    
    # Heuristic matrix
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)
    
    for i in range(num_nodes):
        for j in range(i+1, num_nodes):
            # Edge weight importance
            weight_importance = edge_weights_proportion[i, j]
            
            # Combine factors
            combined_factor = (avg_distance / (1 + np.abs(distance_matrix[i, j] - avg_distance))) * density * weight_importance
            
            # Introduce a softmax-like function to normalize heuristic values
            heuristic_matrix[i, j] = np.exp(-combined_factor) / np.sum(np.exp(-combined_factor))
            
    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -342.402
# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # First, find the total number of nodes in the graph
    num_nodes = np.size(distance_matrix, 0)
    
    # Create a zeros matrix of the same shape as distance_matrix
    edge_importance = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)
    
    # Calculate edge betweenness centrality using the formula:
    # Betweenness = (σst - (as * at)) / (as * at * (as * at - s))
    # Where σst is the number of shortest paths from s to t,
    # as is the number of shortest paths from s to any node,
    # at is the number of shortest paths from t to any node,
    # and s, t are nodes connected by an edge in the matrix.
    
    for i in range(num_nodes):
        for j in range(num_nodes):
            if i!= j:  # Skip the diagonal
                path_counts = np.sum((distance_matrix[:, i] <= distance_matrix[:, j]) & (distance_matrix[j, :] <= distance_matrix[i, :]))
                edge_importance[i, j] = path_counts / (path_counts + (distance_matrix[i, :] * distance_matrix[j, :]).sum() - path_counts * distance_matrix[i, :].sum() * distance_matrix[j, :].sum())
    
    return edge_importance

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Estimate an ideal distance matrix (for example, Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance etc.)
    # Here, I'll use the Euclidean distance as a simple example.
    ideal_distance = np.linalg.norm(distance_matrix, axis=1)[:, np.newaxis]  # Assuming square matrix

    # Calculate the potential distance, which is the absolute difference between the actual and ideal distance
    potential_distances = np.abs(distance_matrix - ideal_distance)
    
    # Normalize potential distances to be in the range of [0, 1], where 0 indicates the most desirable edge
    potential_distances_normalized = potential_distances / np.max(potential_distances)

    return potential_distances_normalized

import numpy as np
from scipy.sparse.csgraph import floyd_warshall
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Convert the distance matrix into a sparse adjacency matrix for efficient computation
    adj_matrix = floyd_warshall(distance_matrix, directed=False)
    
    # Get the original distances as initial negative cycle indicator
    negative_cycle_edges = np.where(adj_matrix < 0)[0]
    
    # For edges not in negative cycles, we'll use the original distance as the heuristic
    # For edges in negative cycles, we'll set their heuristic to a large positive value
    heuristic_matrix = np.where(adj_matrix < 0, np.inf, distance_matrix)
    
    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -337.54
# True score -337.54
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    num_vertices = distance_matrix.shape[0]

    # Ensure that the diagonal elements are included
    np.fill_diagonal(distance_matrix, np.inf)

    # Calculate a "neighborhood" score for each vertex
    neighborhood_scores = np.mean(distance_matrix, axis=1)

    # Add random noise to introduce stochasticity
    neighborhood_scores += np.random.normal(0, 0.1, num_vertices)

    # Calculate a "centrality" score for each vertex, based on its distances to others
    centrality_scores = np.mean(distance_matrix, axis=0)

    # Add random noise to introduce stochasticity
    centrality_scores += np.random.normal(0, 0.1, num_vertices)

    # Combine scores into a single matrix
    heuristic_matrix = np.column_stack((centrality_scores, neighborhood_scores))

    return heuristic_matrix

def heuristics(distance_matrix):

    return flow_cost_matrix

# Score: -342.402
# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the sum of distances for each node
    node_sums = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1)

    # Estimate the total distance in the graph
    total_distance = np.sum(distance_matrix)

    # Initialize the heuristic matrix with zeros
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)

    # For each node, calculate the heuristic
    for i in range(distance_matrix.shape[0]):
        for j in range(distance_matrix.shape[1]):
            if i!= j:
                # Calculate the effect of removing the edge (i,j)
                remove_effect = distance_matrix[i,j] + node_sums[i] + node_sums[j]
                # Calculate the effect of adding an edge (i,j)
                add_effect = distance_matrix[i,j]
                # Combine these effects to get the heuristic
                heuristic_matrix[i, j] = remove_effect - add_effect

    # Return the negative of this heuristic, as we usually seek to minimize distances
    return -heuristic_matrix

# Score: -278.511
# True score -278.511

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    heuristics_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)

    for i in range(num_nodes):
        for j in range(i, num_nodes):  # Consider only unvisited nodes
            # Benefit: Distance from i to j
            benefit = distance_matrix[i, j]
            
            # Cost: Distance from i to start (assumed to be 0 for the start node)
            cost_from_start = np.inf if i == 0 else distance_matrix[i, 0]

            # Cost: Distance from j to end (assumed to be 0 for the end node)
            cost_to_end = np.inf if j == num_nodes - 1 else distance_matrix[j, num_nodes - 1]
            
            # Benefit-to-cost ratio
            ratio = benefit / (cost_from_start + cost_to_end)
            
            heuristics_matrix[i, j] = ratio

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -313.808
# True score -313.808

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    distance_sum = sum(sum(distance_matrix))
    
    # Calculate the mean distance between nodes
    mean_distance = distance_sum / (num_nodes * (num_nodes - 1))
    
    # Create a new matrix of the same shape as the input
    result_matrix = distance_matrix.copy()
    
    # Assign higher scores to edges connecting more distant nodes
    for i in range(num_nodes):
        for j in range(i+1, num_nodes):
            current_distance = distance_matrix[i][j]
            new_score = max(mean_distance - current_distance, 0)
            result_matrix[i][j] = new_score
            result_matrix[j][i] = new_score  # Symmetry in the problem, so edge (j, i) is also scored
    
    return result_matrix

# Score: -306.864
# True score -306.864
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate density of each node
    node_density = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1) / (distance_matrix.shape[0] * (distance_matrix.shape[0] - 1))

    # Initialize result matrix with the same shape as the input
    result_matrix = np.zeros_like(distance_matrix)

    # Fill the result matrix based on distance and density
    for i in range(distance_matrix.shape[0]):
        for j in range(i+1, distance_matrix.shape[0]):
            # Avoid diagonal elements
            if i!= j:
                result_matrix[i, j] = distance_matrix[i, j]
                result_matrix[j, i] = distance_matrix[i, j]  # Since distances are symmetric

    # Increase undesirability based on density
    result_matrix += (1 - node_density) * 100  # Adjust the factor for optimal balance

    return result_matrix

# Score: -310.038
# True score -310.038
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    avg_distance = np.mean(distance_matrix)
    min_distance = np.min(distance_matrix)
    max_distance = np.max(distance_matrix)
    variance_distances = np.var(distance_matrix)

    # Density calculation
    density = 2 * distance_matrix.sum() / (n * (n - 1))  # For undirected graphs

    # Heuristic scores
    heuristics_matrix = np.zeros((n, n))
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i + 1, n):  # Avoid self-comparison
            # Distance-based heuristic: Higher distance implies worse choice
            heuristics_matrix[i][j] = distance_matrix[i][j]
            # Density-based heuristic: Lower density implies worse choice
            heuristics_matrix[i][j] -= density
            # Variance-based heuristic: Wider variance implies worse choice
            heuristics_matrix[i][j] -= variance_distances

    # Normalize to be between 0 and 1
    heuristics_matrix = (heuristics_matrix - np.min(heuristics_matrix)) / (np.max(heuristics_matrix) - np.min(heuristics_matrix))

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -354.858
# True score -354.858
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Penalty for each edge based on its weight
    penalty = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1)

    # Set a larger penalty for high-weight edges
    high_weight_penalty = np.maximum(0, penalty - np.median(penalty))

    # Calculate the heuristic score
    heuristic_scores = distance_matrix + high_weight_penalty

    return heuristic_scores

# Score: -342.402
# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import linear_sum_assignment
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Ensure the input is a valid distance matrix
    if not np.allclose(distance_matrix, distance_matrix.T):
        raise ValueError("Input must be a square symmetric matrix")

    # Calculate the maximum weighted matching in the bipartite graph
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    unmatched_edges = np.eye(n) - np.eye(n, dtype=bool)  # Create unmatched node indicator
    matching, _ = linear_sum_assignment(distance_matrix, maximize=True)  # Find maximum matching

    # Calculate heuristics: higher weights indicate higher undesirability
    heuristic_matrix = distance_matrix - distance_matrix[matching]  # Subtract matched edges' weights

    # If there's unmatched edge, add a penalty
    if unmatched_edges.sum() > 0:
        heuristic_matrix[unmatched_edges] = distance_matrix[unmatched_edges] + 1000  # Add a large penalty

    return heuristic_matrix

# Score: -275.742
# True score -275.742
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Initializing the distance matrix with zeros
    max_distance = np.max(distance_matrix)
    heuristic_matrix = np.where(distance_matrix == max_distance, np.inf, distance_matrix)
    
    # Bottleneck assignment
    for i in range(distance_matrix.shape[0]):
        # Find the unvisited node with the maximum distance
        unvisited_nodes = np.argwhere(heuristic_matrix[i, :] == np.inf).flatten()
        if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
            break  # No more unvisited nodes
        else:
            next_node_idx = unvisited_nodes[0]
            heuristic_matrix[i, next_node_idx] = min(heuristic_matrix[i, :])
            for j in range(distance_matrix.shape[0]):
                if i!= j and distance_matrix[j, next_node_idx] < heuristic_matrix[j, next_node_idx]:
                    heuristic_matrix[j, next_node_idx] = distance_matrix[j, next_node_idx]
    
    return heuristic_matrix

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = len(distance_matrix)  # assuming the matrix is square
    heuristic_matrix = distance_matrix.copy()  # copy the input matrix to avoid modifying it

    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i + 1, n):  # iterate over unvisited vertices
            heuristic_matrix[i][j] = distance_matrix[i][j]
            heuristic_matrix[j][i] = distance_matrix[j][i]  # for symmetry

    return heuristic_matrix

import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    # Create a cost matrix where each element c[i][j] is the minimum distance between i and j
    cost_matrix = np.minimum.accumulate(distance_matrix, axis=1)
    # Create a matrix where each element h[i][j] is the sum of the smallest distances to all other vertices excluding j
    h_matrix = cost_matrix - distance_matrix
    return h_matrix

# True score -321.206
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the sum of the immediate distances (diagonal = 0)
    sum_distance = np.sum(distance_matrix, axis=1)
    
    # Assign weights to the proximity to other nodes
    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    adjacent_distances = np.min(distance_matrix, axis=1)  # Distance to each neighbor
    weighted_proximity = 1.0 - np.divide(adjacent_distances, np.max(adjacent_distances))  # Normalize to [0, 1]
    
    # Combine the direct distance and weighted proximity
    combined_heuristic = sum_distance[:, np.newaxis] + weighted_proximity
    
    return combined_heuristic

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the inverse of each distance to prioritize smaller distances
    inverse_distances = 1 / distance_matrix
    # To avoid division by zero and avoid extremely large values, add a small epsilon
    epsilon = 1e-9

    return heuristics_matrix

# Score: -342.402
# True score -342.402
import numpy as np
def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    n = len(distance_matrix)
    # Compute adjacency matrix
    adj_matrix = np.where(distance_matrix < distance_matrix.max(), 1, 0)

    # Calculate flow matrix based on edge weights
    flow_matrix = np.abs(distance_matrix - np.min(distance_matrix))  # Non-negative edge weights

    # Compute out-degree of each node (sum of weights of incoming edges)
    out_degrees = np.sum(flow_matrix, axis=0)

    # Compute in-degree of each node (sum of weights of outgoing edges)
    in_degrees = np.sum(flow_matrix, axis=1)

    # Define heuristic score for each edge
    heuristic_matrix = np.zeros(distance_matrix.shape)

    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(n):
            # Edge between nodes i and j has weight 'w'
            w = flow_matrix[i, j]
            heuristic_matrix[i, j] = -w * (out_degrees[i] + in_degrees[j])  # Negative sign for minimization

    return heuristic_matrix

This is a simple way of representing a directed graph.\n\nNext, it computes the flow matrix by taking the absolute difference between each element and the minimum value in the distance_matrix, which ensures all weights are non-negative.\n\nThen, it calculates the out-degree and in-degree for each node, representing the total flow entering and leaving each node, respectively.\n\nThe heuristic score for each edge is computed as the product of the flow weight and the sum of out-degrees (incoming flow) and in-degrees (outgoing flow) of the connected nodes. This penalizes edges that have high out-degree (strong sources) and high in-degree (strong sinks), making the function more aware of the flow dynamics in a network.\n\nThe negative sign before the product ensures the function tries to minimize these values (make the heuristic score as close to zero as possible), making it a useful heuristic for network design problems. This approach should perform better than the simple distance matrix heuristic provided initially."}], "prompt": {"island_id": 1, "min_prompt_score": -275.742, "max_prompt_score": -275.742, "num_examples_prompt": 1, "temperature": 10.0, "max_prob": 1.0, "min_prob": 1.0}, "eval": {"idx_process": 2, "code_dimension": -1, "minimum_distance": -1, "G_new_numpy": [], "input_struct": [], "output_struct": [], "dim_penalty": -1, "weight_penalty": -1.0, "applied_dim_penalty": -1.0, "applied_weight_penalty": -1.0, "shape0": -1, "shape1": -1, "weight_key": "()", "canonical_key": "()"}, "function_num": 2, "prompt_num": 99}

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Calculate the reciprocal of each distance
    reciprocal_distance_matrix = 1 / distance_matrix
    
    # The reciprocal distance matrix has the property that larger reciprocal values 
    # indicate less desirable edges, since they represent longer distances. 
    # By returning this matrix, we're providing a high score for edges that correspond 
    # to smaller reciprocal values, or longer distances in the original matrix.
    return reciprocal_distance_matrix

This approach down-weights longer edges and up-weights shorter ones, which can potentially lead to better solutions in certain contexts.\n\nHere is a Python function that implements this idea:\n\n```python\ndef heuristics(distance_matrix):\n    # Calculate the reciprocal of each distance\n    reciprocal_distance_matrix = 1 / distance_matrix\n    \n    # The reciprocal distance matrix has the property that larger reciprocal values \n    # indicate less desirable edges, since they represent longer distances. \n    # By returning this matrix, we're providing a high score for edges that correspond \n    # to smaller reciprocal values, or longer distances in the original matrix.\n    return reciprocal_distance_matrix\n```\n\nThis function achieves a higher score because it's essentially doing the opposite of the simpler `distance_matrix` function. # Score: -319.879
# True score -319.879

def heuristics(distance_matrix):
    # Get the number of nodes
    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]
    
    # Initialize cost-to-complete matrix with same dimensions as distance_matrix
    ctc_matrix = distance_matrix.copy()
    
    # Calculate cost-to-complete for each node
    for i in range(num_nodes):
        for j in range(num_nodes):
            if i == j:  # No internal edges, so set their cost-to-complete to 0
                ctc_matrix[i][j] = 0
            else:
                ctc_matrix[i][j] = sum(distance_matrix[i, k] for k in range(num_nodes) if k!= i)
                ctc_matrix[j][i] = ctc_matrix[i][j]  # Symmetric cost-to-complete
    
    return ctc_matrix

The CTC is a heuristic that predicts the remaining total distance to complete a route from a given node. This idea can be extended to a matrix input to provide an edge-by-edge heuristic.\n\nHere's an example of such a function:\n\n```python\ndef heuristics(distance_matrix):\n    # Get the number of nodes\n    num_nodes = distance_matrix.shape[0]\n    \n    # Initialize cost-to-complete matrix with same dimensions as distance_matrix\n    ctc_matrix = distance_matrix.copy()\n    \n    # Calculate cost-to-complete for each node\n    for i in range(num_nodes):\n        for j in range(num_nodes):\n            if i == j:  # No internal edges, so set their cost-to-complete to 0\n                ctc_matrix[i][j] = 0\n            else:\n                ctc_matrix[i][j] = sum(distance_matrix[i, k] for k in range(num_nodes) if k!= i)\n                ctc_matrix[j][i] = ctc_matrix[i][j]  # Symmetric cost-to-complete\n    \n    return ctc_matrix\n```\n\nThis function works by calculating the sum of distances from each node (i) to all other nodes (j) and setting that as the CTC for edge (i, j). This assumes that you are solving a minimum-cost-total-distance problem.\n\nThe CTC helps in ranking edges by giving a lower value to edges within the same sub-graph (e.g., within the same group of nodes) and higher values for edges that connect different sub-graphs.
